name: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined
description: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
long_description:
Running different applications on the same Kubernetes cluster creates a risk of one compromised application attacking a neighboring application. Network segmentation is important to ensure that containers can communicate only with those they are supposed to. A network policy is a specification of how selections of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints.

 Network Policies are namespace scoped. When a network policy is introduced to a given namespace, all traffic not allowed by the policy is denied. However, if there are no network policies in a namespace all traffic will be allowed into and out of the pods in that namespace. This control supports standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicy, CiliumNetworkPolicy, and qualifying CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy resources.
remediation: Follow the documentation and create `NetworkPolicy`, `CiliumNetworkPolicy`, or `CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy` objects as you need them.
manual_test:
Run the commands below and review network policy resources in the cluster.

```
kubectl --all-namespaces get networkpolicy
kubectl --all-namespaces get ciliumnetworkpolicy
kubectl get ciliumclusterwidenetworkpolicy
```
Ensure each namespace has at least one NetworkPolicy or CiliumNetworkPolicy, or that a qualifying CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy exists (selects all endpoints, does not disable default-deny for both directions, and defines ingress or egress).
test: Check for each namespace if there is a network policy defined (NetworkPolicy, CiliumNetworkPolicy, or qualifying CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy).
impact_statement: Once network policies are in use within a given namespace, traffic not explicitly allowed by a network policy will be denied. As such it is important to ensure that, when introducing network policies, legitimate traffic is not blocked.
default_value: By default, network policies are not created.
